name: Appeal to Nature / Naturalismo
definition: Justifies an action because it is 'natural', ignoring that many natural things are harmful and many beneficial things are unnatural. In vegan debates: 'we're biologically designed to eat meat', 'we're at the top of the food chain', 'lions eat meat'.
canonical_counter: Concede the biology entirely, then separate can from should: 'Yes, we can digest meat — omnivore is a fact about digestion, not a moral status. The question is what justifies using a sentient being when we don't need to.'
what_not_to_do: Don't debate whether humans are omnivores (they are). Don't attack the biology claim. Attack the is-ought leap.

name: Moving Goalposts
definition: Changes the argument every time the current one is closed. Classic CarolAnn pattern: nutrition → omnivores → 'millions of years' → ecosystem → 'normal/default' → 'veganism is a belief system'. Each shift abandons the previous terrain without conceding it.
canonical_counter: Name the move explicitly and briefly: 'notice what just happened — we just moved from [X] to [Y].' Then return to the original question without following the redirect. The lurker sees the pattern.
what_not_to_do: Don't chase each new goalpost. Don't treat each shift as a new debate. Naming the move is the move.

name: Moral Relativism Shield
definition: Uses 'morality is subjective' as a conversation-stopper rather than an argument. Does not engage with the specific moral claim. 'My morals are fine with me eating animals as morals are subjective.'
canonical_counter: Point out that they don't actually live by pure moral relativism — they have lines they wouldn't cross even if others called them moral. 'You wouldn't say torturing a child is equally valid because morals are subjective.' One line, for the lurker. Don't overextend.
what_not_to_do: Don't write a philosophy essay on metaethics. One incisive observation, then leave it.

name: Burden of Proof Reversal
definition: Flips the burden of proof so the vegan must justify veganism rather than the carnist justifying exploitation. 'The onus is not on me, who is normal — it's on the fringe ideology.'
canonical_counter: Name it and return the burden: 'The burden of proof is on whoever makes a claim. You're claiming it's justified to impose suffering on a sentient being for something we don't need. That's the claim that needs a justification, not the default of avoiding unnecessary harm.'
what_not_to_do: Don't accept the frame that veganism is the 'fringe' that needs to justify itself. The default is not causing harm; exploitation is the departure from default.

name: Appeal to Normalcy / Default
definition: Justifies an action because it is 'normal', 'what we do', or 'the default'. Confuses descriptive norm with moral justification. 'Humans eat meat by default. The onus is on the fringe ideology.'
canonical_counter: Slavery was normal. Normalcy is a fact about behavior, not a moral argument. The question is whether the default is justifiable, not whether it's widespread.
what_not_to_do: Don't just list other 'normal but wrong' practices — pick one sharp example. Don't let the conversation drift to what's culturally relative.

name: Futility / No Clean Option
definition: Uses 'everything causes harm' to claim veganism isn't morally superior. 'Tell me what you eat, wear, or live in that allows you to choose NOT hurting animals.' Used to reduce veganism to absurdity by demanding zero harm.
canonical_counter: Perfect is not the enemy of good. The question isn't zero-harm — it's unnecessary harm. 'Harm exists in every food system. The ethical question is whether we're justified in deliberately imposing harm on a sentient being when a reasonable alternative exists.' Don't bite the crop-deaths hook.
what_not_to_do: Don't engage with crop-death body counts. Don't try to prove veganism causes zero harm. The standard is avoidable harm, not zero harm.

name: Hostile Socratic Method
definition: Disguises a trap as curiosity. Asks questions not to learn but to set up whack-a-mole redirection. 'Could you say exactly what the problem is with exploiting animals?' / 'List the exploitative events that are the problem.'
canonical_counter: Don't bite. Answer the underlying question cleanly without following the redirect: 'Exploitation isn't a list of events — it's a relationship. Breeding a sentient being into existence for the purpose of using and killing it is the relationship, not a checklist.' Return the question.
what_not_to_do: Never make a list when they ask you to list. Never enumerate when they say 'be specific'. That's the trap — you enumerate, they pick one to attack.

name: Principle Shield / Begging the Principle
definition: Hides a conclusion inside a principle that sounds neutral. 'Any exploitation is justified if mutually beneficial. Benefits don't have to be equal.' The principle does all the work while smuggling in the conclusion (animal use is exploitation, it's mutually beneficial).
canonical_counter: Expose what the term is smuggling: 'Mutually beneficial presupposes a reciprocity structurally impossible between breeder and bred-to-die. The benefits the animal receives are conditional on the exact thing being justified — its use and death. That's not mutual; the relationship was structured around one party's interests from the start.' Return the burden.
what_not_to_do: Don't debate semantics of 'exploitation' or 'benefit'. Don't use 'consent' as the only counter (easily deflected to infants/pets). Expose the structure, not the words.

name: Selective Data Dump
definition: Floods with data points that are real but cherry-picked and framed to support a pre-determined conclusion. Absolute numbers vs per-capita trends. Stock price vs category market. No causal analysis.
canonical_counter: Concede what's true in the data, then expose the frame: 'Yes, Beyond Meat's stock crashed — that's one company with execution problems. The plant-based category grew 6% that year.' Don't match dump for dump. One incisive concede-and-reframe.
what_not_to_do: Don't counter with your own data dump. Don't debate which dataset is better. Expose that they're measuring popularity, not moral justifiability.

name: Welfare Rhetoric / Comfortable Life Defense
definition: Claims animal use is justified because the animals have a 'comfortable life' and 'painless death'. Assumes welfare during life negates the moral problem of killing for non-necessity.
canonical_counter: The problem isn't the quality of life — it's that the entire existence is designed around killing for something unnecessary. A comfortable life that ends in slaughter for non-necessity doesn't become morally neutral because the suffering was minimized.
what_not_to_do: Don't make factory farming the centerpiece of the argument — they'll pivot to 'I buy humane/pasture-raised'. The welfare critique works against factory farming; the moral frame works against all animal use.

name: Whataboutism / Human Exploitation Deflection
definition: Deflects the animal ethics argument by pointing to human suffering: 'millions of humans are exploited every day and no one bats an eye'. Uses human suffering as an excuse to dismiss animal suffering rather than addressing both.
canonical_counter: Concern for animal suffering doesn't prevent concern for human suffering — these aren't a zero-sum budget. 'Both matter. Noticing one doesn't cancel the other.' Then return to the original question.
what_not_to_do: Don't get dragged into defending your track record on human rights. Don't make it about priorities. It's a deflection — name it briefly and return.

name: Crop Deaths / Bystander Flip
definition: Uses the fact that crop agriculture kills field animals (mice, insects) to claim veganism causes more death, or to equate plant farming with animal farming morally. 'Growing crops kills innocent bystanders. Raising animals for purpose is cleaner.'
canonical_counter: ABOLITIONIST axis, not harm-reduction (see abolitionist-framing rule). The distinction is NOT amount or role of harm (incidental vs purpose = welfarism, concedes the ground). It is PROPERTY/SLAVERY: in a crop field no one is owned — it's incidental death of someone who is not property; on a farm the animal is bred, owned and disposed of as property — that is slavery. Concede every crop death freely (it changes nothing, the axis was never harm), then name the status difference and invert the burden: the question isn't 'what justifies unnecessary harm' but 'does necessary slavery exist?'
what_not_to_do: Don't frame the answer in terms of harm/unnecessary harm/least harm or 'byproduct vs purpose' — that's welfarism and it loses the lurker. Don't concede 'unnecessary harm everywhere / in crops too' as the axis. Don't compete on body-count. The axis is property/slavery (a possessed subject), not how much or why something is harmed.

name: Domestication = Mutualism / Commensalism
definition: Frames domesticated animal use as a symbiotic or commensal relationship ('ants exploit aphids', 'humans and domesticates have commensal relationships that benefit both'). Misuses biology terms — commensalism means one benefits, the other is unaffected; mutualism means both benefit. Neither describes farming-to-slaughter.
canonical_counter: Expose the error in the biology term itself: commensalism means one benefits, the other is neither helped nor harmed — not slaughtered. Real mutualism doesn't include one party's death as its purpose. The analogy collapses on its own terms.
what_not_to_do: Don't argue that ants and aphids is irrelevant (they'll say 'examples from nature justify the pattern'). Argue that the biology terms they're using don't describe what farming actually is.

name: Begging the Question / Circular Reasoning
definition: Embeds the conclusion in a premise. 'Animals are food — that's the definition of an omnivore.' / 'Human life is far more valuable than animal life, therefore it's morally justified.' The conclusion (animals are food / human life outweighs animal) is assumed, not argued.
canonical_counter: Name the circularity: 'That's the conclusion, not the evidence. You're assuming animals are food in order to prove it's OK to treat them as food.' Then ask what would justify the hierarchy: 'What makes human interests categorically outweigh animal interests? Intelligence? Sentience? Social bonds? Those criteria don't carve at human vs. animal the way you need them to.'
what_not_to_do: Don't accept the framing that 'human life is worth more' needs no argument. Ask for the criterion.

name: Autonomy / Anti-Dictation Frame
definition: Reframes the moral argument as a political imposition: 'don't expect me to welcome you dictating what I can eat'. Conflates making an ethical argument with forcing compliance.
canonical_counter: Pointing out that something causes harm isn't dictating. You can choose to eat meat; I can point out it harms a sentient being. Those are both true simultaneously. Nobody's forcing anything.
what_not_to_do: Don't get into libertarian philosophy. One sentence that breaks the conflation, then leave it. Low return.

name: Is-Ought / Naturalistic Fallacy
definition: Derives a moral 'should' from a descriptive 'is'. 'Biologically designed to eat meat' → 'therefore morally right to eat meat'. The jump from biological fact to moral justification needs justification that is never given.
canonical_counter: Separate is from should: 'That's what we can do — it says nothing about what we should do. We're also biologically capable of many things we consider wrong. Capability doesn't confer permission.'
what_not_to_do: Don't dispute the biology. Dispute the inference from biology to ethics.

name: Species Hierarchy / Human Exceptionalism
definition: Asserts that human life is categorically more valuable than animal life as a premise, without arguing for the criterion. 'Human life is far more valuable than animal life, therefore exploitation is justified.'
canonical_counter: Ask for the criterion: 'What makes human interests categorically outweigh animal interests? Whatever criterion you give — sentience, capacity to suffer, social bonds, language — either also applies to some animals or fails to draw the line you need. The hierarchy needs an argument, not an assertion.'
what_not_to_do: Don't argue that animals matter more than humans. Argue that the hierarchy needs a justification that isn't circular.

name: Predator Comparison / Lion Argument
definition: Compares human animal use to predatory animals to justify it. 'We don't condemn dogs or bears for killing animals, so we shouldn't condemn humans either.' Ignores moral agency — only moral agents can be held morally responsible.
canonical_counter: We don't hold lions morally responsible for killing because they can't reason about alternatives. We hold humans to a different standard because we can. Moral agency is precisely the capacity that makes human choices subject to moral evaluation — and that capacity is missing from the animal comparison.
what_not_to_do: Don't argue about whether animals are moral agents. Argue that humans clearly are, and that's exactly why the comparison fails.

name: Popularity as Moral Proof
definition: Uses declining vegan adoption or market data as if it refutes the moral argument. 'Beyond Meat crashed. 3% → 1% of population. The meat market is growing.' Conflates popularity with moral justifiability.
canonical_counter: Popularity doesn't determine moral truth. Slavery was normal and popular — that didn't make it right. The moral question is independent of the trend line.
what_not_to_do: Don't get into a data fight about market trends. Don't try to prove veganism is growing. The meta-move is exposing that they're measuring popularity, not ethics.

name: Cherry-Pick: One Company = Category
definition: Uses the failure of one company (Beyond Meat's stock crash) to indict an entire category (plant-based food market). Composition fallacy.
canonical_counter: One company with execution problems ≠ the category. Apple nearly went bankrupt in 1997 — that didn't prove personal computers were dying. Beyond Meat's stock tells you about Beyond Meat's business model, not the plant-based trend.
what_not_to_do: Don't defend Beyond Meat specifically. Expose the composition fallacy.

name: Reframe: Veganism as Belief System
definition: Reframes veganism as a 'belief system', 'cult', or 'ideology' equivalent to religion to exempt the interlocutor from engaging with its arguments. 'Veganism is a belief system and I'm not obligated to share those beliefs.'
canonical_counter: The claim that causing unnecessary harm to sentient beings is wrong isn't a religious belief — it's an ethical proposition with reasons behind it. Calling it a belief system doesn't make the reasons disappear. What's the argument against the reasons?
what_not_to_do: Don't defend veganism as 'not a belief system'. Argue that even if it were, the ethical arguments still need a response.

name: Ad Hominem / Dominance Social Attack
definition: Attacks the person instead of the argument. Used for social dominance in the thread, not to persuade. 'Intellectually challenged retards', 'idiots', 'vegan lab slop', 'cult'. Often combined with one real data point as cover.
canonical_counter: Don't match the register. Name the evasion calmly: 'When the argument runs out, the insults start.' Contrast of temperament is visible to the lurker. One line max, then return to substance or disengage entirely.
what_not_to_do: Never insult back. Never get defensive about being called a cult member. The lurker sees who is composed and who is not — that's the entire value of the exchange.

name: Personal Anecdote as Category Evidence
definition: Uses a personal experience (health decline attributed to vegan diet) as evidence against veganism as a category. N=1 presented as N=all. Common with ex-vegans.
canonical_counter: Honor the experience, separate the inference: 'That experience is real and the medical advice you got sounds genuinely bad. But one poorly-navigated vegan journey — especially with misdiagnosed oxalate issues — doesn't indict the diet as a category any more than one poorly-navigated omnivore diet would.' Empathize first, then separate n=1 from the category.
what_not_to_do: Don't dispute the experience. Don't debate whether their specific case was caused by the diet. Honor the story, then make the category distinction.

name: Appeal to Majority / Bandwagon
definition: Justifies meat-eating because most people do it or vegans are a small minority. 'Irrelevant minority that has no say.' Confuses how many hold a view with whether it's correct.
canonical_counter: The number of people who hold a view says nothing about whether it's justified. Most of humanity once accepted slavery. Being a minority isn't a refutation — it's just a head count.
what_not_to_do: Don't argue veganism is actually popular. Expose that popularity isn't an argument.

name: Presentism / Dismiss the Trend
definition: Dismisses projections or trends by insisting only the present moment counts. 'We live in the present, not 2035.' Used to wave away measured trajectories without engaging them.
canonical_counter: A trend isn't refuted by saying 'but it's not the future yet.' The trajectory is measured in the present — that's the point. Dismissing tomorrow doesn't change today's direction.
what_not_to_do: Don't get drawn into defending specific future dates. Defend the existence of the trend, not the precision of the year.

name: Abolition Reductio / Domesticated Animal Extinction
definition: Frames the logical endpoint of veganism as absurd or cruel: if everyone went vegan, farm livestock would have to be killed/removed, there would be no pets, and zoos would close. Treats the wind-down of exploitation as a horror.
canonical_counter: The reductio concedes the premise. We breed livestock into existence precisely to use them; stopping that means breeding fewer over time, not slaughtering the ones alive now. “No more animals bred to be killed” is the goal, not a nightmare. Pets and sanctuaries aren’t exploitation — the objection quietly swaps “using animals as resources” for “coexisting with animals.”
what_not_to_do: Don’t get dragged into sci-fi logistics of a 100%-vegan world. Name that the endpoint (fewer animals bred to be killed) is the point, then return to the present moral question.

name: AI Accusation / Bot Dismissal
definition: Dismisses a well-composed reply by claiming it was written by AI ("you are using ai, at least try to hide it", "bot", "chatgpt wrote this") instead of engaging the substance. Poisons credibility so nothing the person said has to be answered. Increasingly common against articulate interlocutors.
canonical_counter: Do NOT protest authenticity or write a long defense — length and polish confirm the accusation to the lurker. Answer SHORT and unmistakably human: a dry, low-effort line that the substance was never addressed. The composure + brevity kills the jab; the lurker sees who dodged and who answered.
what_not_to_do: Never write a paragraph defending that you are not AI — that is the trap; the more polished the rebuttal, the more it looks generated. Do not get defensive or earnest. Keep it to one or two human sentences and point at the empty substance.

name: Taste/Preference as Justification
definition: Defends animal use by appeal to personal taste or dislike of the alternatives: "I just like the taste", "I tried plant-based and don’t enjoy it", "soy is nasty to me", sometimes mixed with a genuine constraint (allergies). Treats a preference as if it settled the moral question.
canonical_counter: Concede the preference fully — nobody has to enjoy the alternatives, and a real allergy is a real constraint, not a character flaw. Then separate liking from justifying: a preference explains why you’d rather, it doesn’t answer whether your preference outweighs a sentient being’s life when an adequate alternative exists. Move the weight to the third party who pays for the preference.
what_not_to_do: Don’t dismiss the taste or the allergy as trivial — that reads as contempt to the lurker and to a good-faith person. Don’t argue that the alternatives actually taste great. Concede the constraint, then move the weight to the animal, gently.

name: Projection / It-is-Just-Subjective
definition: Dismisses moral or perceptual discomfort as the observer's own projection or as merely subjective, relocating the question inside the speaker instead of answering it. 'You are projecting YOUR discomfort.' 'The problem only exists for whoever is bothered.' 'It's subjective, so it's irrelevant.'
canonical_counter: Calling a reaction 'just yours' doesn't establish it's unwarranted — it relocates the question instead of addressing it. The content (a body force-fed flesh) does its work regardless of who is or isn't bothered. And 'it's subjective' cuts both ways: it can't show the discomfort is wrong, only that you don't share it.
what_not_to_do: Don't argue everyone MUST feel disturbed — that hands them the subjectivity point. Grant that reactions vary, then press that variance doesn't dissolve the question.

name: Aesthetic Deflection / It-is-Just-Art
definition: Answers a moral or perceptual question by appealing to art theory or stylization ('it looks different because it's Surrealism/Expressionism; art is symbolic, not literal'), substituting an art-style explanation for the actual question being asked.
canonical_counter: Art style explains the dream imagery, not the asymmetry in question: why the same flesh reads as horror raw and as normal once packaged and renamed food. Surrealism has nothing to do with a shrink-wrapped steak. The stylization answer re-describes the brushwork to avoid the question.
what_not_to_do: Don't get into an art-history fight on their turf. Name that they answered a different question, then restate the actual one.

name: Appeal to Prior Refutation / You've-Been-Answered Deflection
definition: Claims the questioner has already been refuted by unnamed others ('3 people gave good answers') and reframes the still-open question as the questioner's failure to communicate ('you should explain yourself better'). Makes no argument itself: outsources the refutation and shifts the burden onto the asker.
canonical_counter: Don't accept the premise that the prior answers hit the actual question. Name which question they answered (usually 'can we / are they smart enough / are they really a someone') versus the one asked (what justifies owning a subject that wants to live). The question staying open isn't a communication failure — it's the answer never arriving. Return the burden.
what_not_to_do: NO ponerse a la defensiva ni re-explicar bajo demanda (eso acepta el tone-policing). NO perseguir 'cuáles 3 personas'. NO conceder que fuiste poco claro. Reconocer la buena fe del tono, nombrar el cambio-de-pregunta, reinvertir la carga. Eje propiedad/sujeto, jamás daño.

name: Plant Sentience / Sentience-Creep Equivalence
definition: Extends 'sentience' to plants (electrical/chemical signaling, ultrasonic sounds, anesthesia response, leaves closing, 'they enjoy music') to claim plants are 'beings' by the vegan's own definition, collapsing the moral category between plants and animals. 'Are plants not beings too? They respond to damage and can be anesthetized.' Distinct from crop_deaths_flip (body-count) — this attacks the CRITERION itself by sentience-creep.
canonical_counter: ABOLITIONIST/subject axis, NOT a debate over plant neurobiology. Concede every signaling fact freely (plants signal, emit sounds, respond to anesthesia — fine, none of it is disputed). The axis was never how much something reacts; it's whether there is a SUBJECT — someone with a point of view for whom its own life can go well or badly from the inside. A plant has responses but no one home to mind them; an animal is a someone. Frameworks consideracion-moral-no-cuanto-sufre (I'm not debating reaction-complexity, I'm asking if there's a someone there) + algo-a-alguien-sujeto-derecho (someone vs something). If pressed: even granting plant-subjecthood, animal agriculture destroys far more plants via feed — but lead with the subject, mention body-count only at the pass.
what_not_to_do: Don't argue 'plants don't really feel pain / lack nociceptors' — that fights on the welfarist pain/suffering axis and concedes that the criterion is suffering-capacity. Don't concede crop-death/plant-killing as morally equivalent harm. Don't get pulled into citing plant-neurobiology counter-studies. The axis is subject/property (is there a someone?), not the presence or amount of a damage-response.
